items.rationale: Seen as the next foundational unlock — scaling (rollups) plus privacy/encryption that broadens blockchain utility; compared in importance to smart contracts themselves.

citations.quote: Zero Knowledge Proofs will completely transform the web3 tech stack in the coming years.
Encryption & privacy will be essential for blockchains to broaden their utility and gain widespread adoption.
the implications of zero knowledge proofs being a thing may be as dramatic as the implications of smart contracts

items.rationale: Framed as a general digital primitive like the website; value from community, true ownership, and creator take-rates far below web2. View matured from PFP hype toward durable collectibles/utility.
items.age_note: art/PFP hype 2021; reframed toward Pokemon-style collectibles + utility/licensing through 2022

citations.quote: tokens are better thought of as a new digital primitive, analogous to the website
the long run digital collectibles business model will look more like Pokemon cards than the ultra-scarcity pervasive in NFTs today
Today we're thrilled to be launching a slate of open sourced 'Can't be Evil' NFT licenses.

items.rationale: Core thesis; post-FTX the house argues DeFi's transparency/trustlessness is the answer to CeFi failures. Active investing (Morpho, lending primitives).

citations.quote: Do the collapses of FTX, Celsius, and Terra signal the end of crypto? No, but they do show why we need #DeFi.
We believe on-chain lending is an essential DeFi primitive

items.rationale: Decentralizing layers above base chain/smart contracts — clients, social graphs — to escape platform 'rug' risk and enable credibly neutral networks.

citations.quote: Farcaster is the neutral platform we wanted all along.
The decentralization of social media is going to be very obvious to the masses in retrospect.

items.rationale: Post-merge Ethereum viewed as superior base layer; demand for blockspace will outpace supply, so rollups/L2s/L3s and execution diversity are the path to scale.

citations.quote: Once it's done, I believe Ethereum will be a far superior blockchain than it was before.
It seems L3s (also called hyper chains) can solve many of the same challenges as L1 appchains

items.rationale: Token incentives bootstrap real-world supply-side networks (telecom, storage); the key test is verification cost vs incumbent margin.

citations.quote: Helium's use of crypto incentives spurred the world's fastest-growing wireless network
The essential question when considering building a DePIN network: Verification cost < incumbent margin

items.rationale: Newest frontier: crypto gives AI ownership/sovereignty and can incentivize crowdsourced data/models/compute. Early and exploratory but intensifying into 2023.

citations.quote: AI = how machines can think, crypto = how machines can own, AI + crypto = how machines can think on their own
AI + crypto = collective AI

items.rationale: Still seen as a foundational new coordination primitive, but expectations explicitly revised down toward slower 'governance' bodies rather than dynamic startups.
items.age_note: utopian 2021; cooled to 'city governments, not startups' by mid-2022

citations.quote: DAOs are a better, digitally-native mechanism for human coordination.
Most DAOs should be viewed as city governments, not dynamic startups.

items.rationale: Post-FTX emphasis on moving past 'not your keys, not your crypto' with MPC/multisig custody that keeps ownership while improving UX/security.
items.age_note: investment thesis June 2022; reinforced by Nov 2022 CeFi collapses

citations.quote: With the rise of smart contract wallets, multi-sig, and multi-party compute protocols we can finally advance past 'not your keys, not your crypto.'

items.rationale: Treated as an open research frontier: better NFT sale auctions, indirect/liquid democracy for DAOs, and shared-security/restaking economics.

citations.quote: I generally prefer clearing-price auctions to descending pay-as-bid auctions
it's going to have to involve a lot of indirect democracy

items.rationale: The dominant, most-restated concern: four years without constructive guidance, enforcement instead of rulemaking, which the house argues breeds arbitrage and harms good actors. Tone hardened sharply post-FTX.

citations.quote: Regulation-by-enforcement was never supposed to work.
Chairman Gensler has continued to demonstrate that he only knows how to regulate through sporadic enforcement cases.
the SEC should end its regulation by enforcement strategy

items.rationale: Centralized custodial trust is the real failure vector; collapses are framed as 'not decentralized' and preventable with existing law. Expect bad-faith profiteers to return.

citations.quote: FTX/FTT - Not decentralized.
demonstrating unequivocally why trust inherently needs to be regulated differently than trustless
the profiteers will be back

items.rationale: Applying compliance obligations to smart contracts/DAOs would make decentralization impossible; regulation should hit the app/client layer, like regulating Gmail not SMTP.

citations.quote: We can't regulate protocols and keep them decentralized and autonomous. But we can regulate businesses using those protocols.
Why we should regulate web3 apps, not protocols.

items.rationale: Bad collateral (not the algorithm) is the root failure; uncollateralized designs are dismissed outright while well-collateralized decentralized stablecoins are defended.

citations.quote: Uncollateralized stablecoins are basically just meme coins with no potential upside.
bad collateral is the root cause of recent collapses like Terra, not 'algorithmic stablecoins.'

items.rationale: Direct democracy doesn't scale; apathy lets small groups capture proposals. Need indirect/representative delegation, oversight and a 'free press' layer.

citations.quote: you simply can't govern through direct democracy at any kind of meaningful scale
small groups of people who can benefit from getting certain things passed are going to start pushing to pass things

items.rationale: Regulatory uncertainty pushes development and developers out of the US, reducing US oversight and increasing risk to US users.

citations.quote: These are powerful numbers showing web3 development moving outside the US.
Bad regs -> down US development -> down US oversight -> up Risk to US.

items.rationale: Selling tokens to US persons for fundraising and hype-driven roadmaps remain the clearest legal risk; the house warns against repeating 2017–18.

citations.quote: Don't sell tokens to U.S. persons for fundraising purposes.
transparent attempts create hype cycles that are reminiscent of the ICO/unregistered securities offerings of 2017/2018

items.rationale: Deployed SNARKs running at ~80 bits of security risk eroding trust and loss of funds if performance is prioritized over soundness.

citations.quote: if we prioritize performance over security, we may cause people to lose trust in the technology

items.rationale: Custody is a liability; exchanges and multi-sig bridges keep getting hacked, and 'proof of reserves' disclosures (e.g. Binance) are dismissed as easily gamed.

citations.quote: exchanges just keep getting hacked
Binance's system is useless and easily exploited by them.

items.rationale: App-store gatekeepers cripple or tax native crypto experiences, a structural drag on mainstream UX.

citations.quote: Apple will cripple you, block you, or take your lunch if you try to offer a native experience.

items.rationale: Clear developer-mindshare leader; post-merge viewed as more secure/efficient and the settlement foundation for rollups and the broader stack.

citations.quote: Ethereum is the clear leader. Its overwhelming mindshare among web3 devs
I believe Ethereum will be a far superior blockchain than it was before.

items.rationale: Held up as a genuinely trustless, self-sustaining protocol with a treasury; a16z is an active, engaged governance participant.

citations.quote: Uniswap is very much real and is infinitely scalable without a single developer having to lift another finger.

items.rationale: Valued as risk tokens that underwrite their protocols; MakerDAO cited as a case where token governance actually works.

citations.quote: the role of MKR and AAVE/COMP as the risk token that underwrites the protocol
MakerDAO is probably one of the examples where this actually works.

items.rationale: Defended as legitimate decentralized stablecoins distinct from Terra; well-collateralized and a capital-efficiency unlock for the ecosystem.

citations.quote: The bill just doesn't address decentralized and algorithmic stablecoins like DAI and Frax.
The difference between endogenous and exogenous collateral is pretty significant.

items.rationale: Expected DeFi integrations to drive new demand for staking derivatives around the merge.
items.age_note: stated 2021 pre-merge; not restated as a token call since

citations.quote: a lot of the integrations may come before the merge, which will generate a new wave of demand for stETH

items.rationale: Praised as the model for a fair token launch and a building block for interoperable web3 identity/login.

citations.quote: @ensdomains and $ENS should be a case study on how to conduct a great token launch.

items.rationale: Collapse blamed on endogenous/under-collateralization; repeatedly cited as the canonical example of bad-collateral failure and 'not decentralized.'

citations.quote: LUNA - Not decentralized
Terra being extremely undercollateralized

items.rationale: Long viewed with skepticism in legal circles; collapse framed as a centralized, preventable fraud regulators ignored.

citations.quote: FTT was widely viewed with skepticism in the legal community
FTX/FTT - Not decentralized.

items.rationale: Concern is legal, not economic: 'utility token' / centralized issuer profiles make them weak test cases and securities-risk exemplars.

citations.quote: Ripple - Not decentralized.
the final nail in the coffin of the 'utility token' approach for most projects

items.rationale: Endorsed an external view that as a meme it lacks fundamental economic reason to thrive.

citations.quote: its provenance as a meme leaves it without much of a fundamental economic reason to thrive.

risk_regime.rationale: Structurally bullish, long-horizon builders: in May 2022 they declared the 'golden era of web3' and raised Crypto Fund 4, and through the 2022–23 bear market the house frames the downturn as a feature — 'Bear markets are nice as they prolong the good faith innovation phase' (2023-02-03) — and repeats 'keep building.' But the posture is tactically defensive, not euphoric: near-term they are preoccupied with regulatory hostility (Gensler/SEC), CeFi contagion (FTX/Celsius/Terra), and offshoring risk. So the bet is structural conviction in the technology paired with caution on markets, intermediaries, and US policy.

notes: Corpus is multiple distinct a16z crypto voices (e.g., Dixon-style 'why web3' theses, Lazzarin/Yahya infra & token-design takes, a Kominers-style NFT/market-design strand, a Roughgarden/Boneh academic-cryptography strand, and a heavy legal/policy strand on DAOs and SEC regulation). I treated their aggregate as 'the house.' Recency weighting mattered most for: (1) regulation, which escalated from 2021 advocacy to open hostility toward Gensler by Feb 2023 — reported as the current stance; (2) DAOs, where 2021 utopianism was explicitly tempered to 'city governments, not startups' by mid-2022; (3) NFTs, where 2021 PFP exuberance shifted to a durable 'Pokemon-card' collectibles framing. Token-level views are sparse and cautious — the team rarely endorses specific tokens and frames most token 'concerns' as legal/securities risk rather than price calls; several token entries (stETH, ENS, SHIB) are persisted (stated once long ago, not restated). AI×crypto and restaking/EigenLayer are genuinely emergent (late-2022 onward) and partly extrapolated from direction-of-travel statements. The single most consistent, highest-intensity theme across the whole corpus is 'regulate apps not protocols / decentralization as the legal and technical dividing line.'
